title: Day 1: Auction Fundamentals
concepts: Second-price auction: winner pays second highest bid, not their own – encourages truthful bidding.
VCG mechanism generalizes second-price to multiple slots with externality pricing – each advertiser pays harm caused to others.
Bid = value × probability – advertiser value per outcome multiplied by predicted action rate (pCTR, pCVR).
Reserve prices set minimum clearing price per auction to protect user experience and publisher yield.
Auction density affects competition – more eligible ads increases price pressure and improves match quality.
Welfare maximization objective balances advertiser value, user relevance, and platform long-term health.

quiz.question: In a second-price auction, what does the winner pay?
quiz.explanation: Second-price incentivizes truthful bidding because payment is decoupled from your stated bid – you pay the next best alternative.

quiz.statement: VCG pricing means each winner pays the total harm their presence causes to other participants' total value.
quiz.why: True. VCG computes externality – difference in total welfare with vs without you – generalizing second-price logic to multi-slot.

quiz.front: Why is bid decomposed as value × probability?
quiz.back: Advertisers care about outcomes (clicks, conversions), not impressions. We predict p(action|impression) and multiply by advertiser-declared value per action to get expected value bid in impression auction. Separates prediction from valuation.

quiz.question: What is the primary role of reserve prices?
options: Guarantee every auction has a winner
Set floor to protect UX and ensure minimum quality / yield
quiz.explanation: Reserves filter low-value ads, protect user experience from irrelevant ads, and ensure publisher doesn't sell inventory below opportunity cost.

quiz.statement: Higher auction density generally leads to lower clearing prices.
quiz.why: False – more competition pushes clearing price up toward true value, improving efficiency and yield (though diminishing returns exist).
